subject: Chemistry
title: Three Mile Island: A Partial Meltdown
description: Learn Three Mile Island: A Partial Meltdown in one minute.

hook.text: In 1979, a stuck valve and misleading instruments caused a partial meltdown at Three Mile Island in Pennsylvania.

buildup.text: A valve stuck open, draining coolant water. Operators saw a signal saying the valve was closed—it showed command, not status.

discovery.text: Without coolant, fuel rods overheated and partially melted. Half the reactor core was damaged before anyone realized.

twist.text: Little radiation escaped—containment held. But public fear surged. No new US reactors were ordered for 30 years.

climax.text: The accident proved that even 'safe' designs can fail from simple human-interface errors. Better displays saved future plants.

punchline.text: A stuck valve and a misleading light. Small design flaws caused a nuclear crisis.

quiz.question: What fundamental problem caused the Three Mile Island accident?
options: A valve stuck open and instruments showed the wrong status to operators
An earthquake damaged the reactor cooling system
Workers accidentally withdrew all control rods at once
